title: Pine Library Book Cart
summary: A rolling A-frame book cart with cut-out carry handles — plain 1x boards, castered, sized to slide two shelves of books between rooms.
description:
This cart takes its cue from real library book carts: two solid side panels with a cut-out handle, two shelves between them, and casters underneath so a full load of books moves without anyone lifting it. The side panels are the one place worth spending extra care — cut the handle opening and round the corners on the first panel, then use it as a pattern to trace and cut the second, so both sides actually match instead of being two independent attempts at the same shape.

The bottom shelf can mount two ways, and the plan leaves the choice open: flush at the very bottom of the sides, or raised on 1x2 supports for a bit more toe clearance when rolling the cart around a corner. Either works structurally; it's a preference call about how low the cart profile needs to be.

The top shelf overhangs its supports slightly and gets screwed down from above once the top-shelf support strip is in place — that support strip is what turns a wobbly top shelf into one that can carry a real stack of books without flexing. Casters go on last, screwed into the bottom shelf with whatever hardware ships with them; buy locking casters if this cart will spend time near stairs or a sloped floor.

tools.note: Cuts the handle opening in the side panels.
Alternative to the self-tapping screws for the main joints.

materials.name: 1x2, 6 ft
materials.note: Bottom shelf trim, or optional bottom shelf supports.

materials.name: Self-tapping star bit screws, 2", or pocket hole screws, 1-1/4"
Caster wheels, with mounting screws
materials.note: Not in the source shopping list, but required per the final step — locking casters recommended near stairs or slopes.

materials.name: Wood filler, glue, and finishing supplies

cutList.note: Cut the handle opening and round the corners on one, then trace it as a pattern for the second.

steps.title: Cut the side panels
steps.body: On the two side pieces, cut out the handle opening and round the corners if you'd like. Cut the first panel carefully, then use it as a pattern to trace and cut the second — this is what makes both sides actually match rather than being two independent attempts at the same shape. Lightly sand the cut edges of both now, while they're easy to reach.

steps.title: Attach the bottom shelf
steps.body: Attach the bottom shelf to the sides with either 3/4" pocket holes and 1-1/4" pocket hole screws, or 2" self-tapping star bit screws. Mount it flush at the bottom of the sides, or elevated on 1x2 supports underneath for a bit more clearance rolling around corners — either works.
materials: Self-tapping star bit screws, 2", or pocket hole screws, 1-1/4"

steps.title: Trim the bottom shelf
steps.body: Attach the bottom shelf trim pieces to the sides of the bottom shelf with 1-1/4" brad nails and glue.
materials: Wood filler, glue, and finishing supplies

steps.title: Add the top shelf support
steps.body: Measure and mark the location for the top shelf support and attach with either pocket holes or 2" self-tapping screws. This strip is what keeps a fully loaded top shelf from flexing.
materials: Self-tapping star bit screws, 2", or pocket hole screws, 1-1/4"

steps.title: Attach the back
steps.body: Attach the top back piece to the top shelf support with screws, or 1-1/4" brad nails and glue.
materials: Wood filler, glue, and finishing supplies

steps.title: Add the top shelf
steps.body: Place the top shelf on top of the shelf support. Mark its position and screw in place with 2" screws and glue.
materials: Self-tapping star bit screws, 2", or pocket hole screws, 1-1/4"
Wood filler, glue, and finishing supplies

steps.title: Mount the casters
steps.body: Attach the caster wheels to the base of the cart with the screws included with them. Sand, fill any visible fasteners, and finish the whole cart before loading it with books.

images.alt: Rolling wooden library book cart with a cut-out carry handle
